articles.summary: Unlike nearly all other arts, architecture is inherently public and shared. That means that buildings should be designed to be agreeable – easy to like – not to be unpopular works of genius.

articles.summary: Hundreds of thousands of people die from malaria each year, but it took 141 years to develop a vaccine for it. Advance market commitments could speed things up next time.

articles.summary: Houston was notorious for its sprawl. But it has seen a gentle density revolution since the 1990s. Allowing neighborhoods to opt out of citywide reforms was crucial in its transformation.

articles.title: How Mexico built a state
articles.summary: Building a state is not a matter of copying first world institutions. It is a tough process of deals and compromises. 19th century Mexico is a good example.

articles.title: Understanding the Baby Boom

articles.title: Age of Invention: Does History have a Replication Crisis?
articles.summary: Back in 2011, the field of psychology went into crisis. Some of the most famous and widely-cited experimental results could not be replicated by others. articles.author: Anton Howes

articles.title: Why Britain doesn’t build

articles.title: Thomas Edison, tinkerer

articles.title: How DC densified

articles.title: Olivine weathering

articles.title: Taming the stars
articles.summary: Cheap, safe nuclear power is possible, but is all but prohibited in most Western countries. A regulatory sandbox for fission could shake us out of our regulatory sclerosis.

articles.title: The end of acid rain

articles.title: Every grain of rice
articles.summary: As climate change threatens crop yields, we need a second Green Revolution – one that, this time, is driven by genetic engineering.

articles.title: Gentle Density

articles.title: Using Large Language Models to generate democracy scores: An experiment

articles.title: On the origins of empathy for other species

articles.title: Building back faster

articles.title: Every generator is a policy failure

It has £800m and the freedom to make big bold bets on the scientific trajectories it thinks will benefit Britain – and change the future. We interviewed chief executive Ilan Gur and chairman Matt Clifford, to better understand what inspired ARIA and how they aim to make it a success.

articles.title: The story of VaccinateCA

articles.title: Developing the science of science
articles.summary: International development was revolutionized by experiments and evaluations of its methods. Meta-science can learn from it.

articles.title: Pandemic prevention as fire-fighting
articles.summary: Fire has almost disappeared as a cause of death in the developed world. A similar approach could do the same for infectious diseases.

articles.title: Advancing antivenom
articles.summary: Snakebites kill between 80,000 and 140,000 people every year. Better antivenom should be a high priority – thankfully new technology can help.

articles.title: History is in the making
articles.summary: Though we tend to see history as just one political event after another, it’s technology and ideas, not politics, that change our lives the most. History should reflect that.

articles.title: AI from Superintelligence to ChatGPT
articles.summary: Breakthroughs in artificial intelligence are forcing skeptics to eat their words. We should take its risks seriously too.

articles.title: The elements of scientific style

articles.title: Anti-growth safetyism

articles.title: Interview: J. Storrs Hall on getting lost in stagnation

articles.title: Making energy too cheap to meter
articles.summary: The great slowdown began when we started rationing energy. Restarting progress means getting energy that is so abundant that it’s almost free.

articles.title: Nanotechnology’s spring
articles.summary: Nanotechnology sometimes sounds as much like science fiction as artificial intelligence once did. But the problems holding it back seem solvable, and some of the answers may lie inside our own bodies.

articles.title: There was no great stagnation
articles.summary: We may not have flying cars but we do have incredible information technology. We’re mismeasuring the huge benefits it is bringing.

articles.title: Planes, claims and automobiles
articles.summary: Americans famously love to sue one another. Are out of control product liability lawsuits the to blame for the crash of the personal aviation industry?

articles.title: A look at Cuba’s grassroots internet
articles.summary: A documentary from Stripe Press that follows the evolution of a rudimentary gaming network between friends in Cuba into a DIY internet that serviced most of the island.

articles.title: The decline and fall of the British economy
articles.summary: When America’s economy overtook Britain’s a century ago, it remade the world order. How it happened is still debated.

articles.title: The Maintenance Race

articles.title: Real peer review has never been tried
articles.summary: Outdated forms of peer review create bottlenecks that slow science. But in a world where research can now circulate rapidly on the Internet, we need to develop new ways to do science in public.

articles.title: We don’t have a hundred biases, we have the wrong model
articles.summary: Behavioral economics has identified dozens of cognitive biases that stop us from acting ‘rationally’. But instead of building up a messier and messier picture of human behavior, we need a new model.

articles.title: Reclaiming the roads
articles.summary: Until recently, roads were shared between a messy mix of cyclists, stagecoaches, carts, horses, and pedestrians, with no dominant user.

articles.title: Age of the bacteriophage
articles.summary: Bacteriophages – viruses that infect bacterial cells – were almost forgotten in the age of antibiotics. Now as bacterial resistance grows, they may return to help us in our hour of need.

articles.title: Why innovation prizes fail
articles.summary: History’s most famous innovation prize—the longitude rewards—is misunderstood. Innovation prizes are best at promoting refinements, not revolutions.

articles.title: Local warming
articles.summary: Gas heating is bad for the environment. But home-built heat pumps aren’t perfect either.

articles.title: London’s lost ringways
articles.summary: A monstrous plan to build major motorways through some of London’s greatest neighborhoods fell apart. But the price was the birth of the NIMBY movement, and a permanent ceiling on Britain’s infrastructure ambitions.

articles.title: How polyester bounced back
articles.summary: Polyester went from being the world’s most hated fabrics to one of its favorites. It’s so successful that many people don’t even realize they’re wearing polyester today.

articles.title: Scientific slowdown is not inevitable
articles.summary: Some think of advances in science and technology through the metaphor of low-hanging fruit: we “picked” the easy ones, and the rest will be very difficult.

articles.title: Why we duel
articles.summary: Duels can be brutal and even lethal. But duels emerged in societies around the world for an important reason: to control and manage violence, not just to celebrate it.

articles.title: Why Ireland’s housing bubble burst
articles.summary: Ireland’s housing bubble and bust has become emblematic of what not to do in housing debates around the world. The only problem is nobody agrees what actually went wrong.

articles.title: Womb for improvement
articles.summary: Pregnancy can be arduous, painful and for some women impossible. New technology may allow more women to have children, and save the lives of more prematurely born infants. How do we get there?

articles.title: Why skyscrapers are so short
articles.summary: The height of skyscrapers is limited by physical, economic and regulatory barriers, but we should want to overcome them and build taller. Here’s how we can do it.

articles.title: Parenting as a public good
articles.summary: Society has free-ridden on women for millennia, benefiting from the children they’ve had while bearing few of the costs. But as women have gained other options, birth rates have fallen.

articles.title: Against the survival of the prettiest
articles.summary: Many modern buildings put up today seem uglier than traditional ones around them. Some say this is because we’ve torn down the ugly old buildings, and only see the survivors. Are they right?

articles.title: Who cares about plagiarism?
articles.summary: Plagiarism is unforgivable in academia but it’s not plagiarism itself that should trouble us. It’s carelessness and a lack of originality.

articles.title: Natalism for progressives
articles.summary: Without new humans, growth will slow, and we will be less likely to reach the stars. But pro-natalism has been captured by a range of unsavory voices. There is an alternative.

articles.title: How trust undermines science
articles.summary: Our success is based on scientific discovery, so it’s not surprising how much faith we put into it. But we now trust science so implicitly that our trust undermines the institution itself.

articles.title: Better eats
articles.summary: The kitchen of 2020 looks mostly the same as that of 1960. But what we do in it has changed dramatically, almost entirely for the better—due to a culture of culinary innovation.

articles.title: Buyers of first resort
articles.summary: How do technologies get off the ground? As well as seed funding, many of the best technologies require Buyers of First Resort, which buy products until they improve enough to get to efficient scale.

articles.title: The housing theory of everything
articles.summary: Western housing shortages do not just prevent many from ever affording their own home. They also drive inequality, climate change, low productivity growth, obesity, and even falling fertility rates.

articles.title: The future of weight loss
articles.summary: We have eradicated smallpox, cured many bacterial diseases, and invented a vaccine for Covid-19 within the year. But for a very long time we haven’t had a single good treatment for obesity.

articles.title: Asteroid spotting
articles.summary: Could an asteroid wipe out human civilisation like it may have eliminated the dinosaurs? Big asteroids come along extremely rarely and our monitoring systems are effective and well funded.

articles.title: Why didn’t suicides rise during Covid?
articles.summary: Covid-19 brought death, suffering and financial straits, so it was unsurprising that depression rose around the world. But when the data came in, we found suicide did not – and it’s a mystery why.

articles.title: How we fixed the ozone layer
articles.summary: The story behind humanity’s greatest environmental success is too rarely told and too often taken for granted. This is how humanity fixed the ozone layer and why it matters.

articles.title: Why Tesla bought bitcoin
articles.summary: Everybody loves to hate Bitcoin. Yet big business is spending hundreds of millions on it, helping to drive the price higher and higher. It’s easy to dismiss that as a marketing fad.

articles.title: Burying the lead
articles.summary: Researchers have known for decades that lead poisoning damages brains and worsens crime, but millions of Americans still drink contaminated water every day. Here’s how we can fix that.

articles.title: This is what peak culture looks like
articles.summary: Is the popularity of the Marvel Cinematic Universe a sign of art in decline? It’s common for people to assert that film, art, music and literature are getting worse. This is why they’re wrong.

articles.title: Interview: Ted Nordhaus on ecomodernism
articles.summary: Are technology and the environment friends or foes? In this wide-ranging conversation, we discuss climate policy, activism and ecomodernism with Ted Nordhaus.

articles.title: We don’t know how to fix science
articles.summary: The conversation around science is full of ideas for reform, but how do we know which ones will be effective? To find out what works, we need to apply the scientific method to science itself.

articles.title: The speed of science
articles.summary: Critics of scientific reform say that transparency comes at the cost of speed. What can disciplines learn from each other to break away from this crisis?

articles.title: How I learned to stop worrying and love the debt
articles.summary: Protecting people’s health during the pandemic will increase debt, but the economic consensus is that we shouldn’t be concerned.

articles.title: The great reinforcer
articles.summary: Alongside all the successes of science in the Covid era, the pandemic has also sparked an outbreak of viral misinformation and sloppy research, revealing the glaring flaws in our scientific system.

articles.title: Why tech cannot escape expensive housing
articles.summary: As rental prices continue to climb in San Francisco, tech firms have looked to relocate in other cities. Without major housing reforms, the next Silicon Valley will face the same fate.

articles.title: How Covid brought the future back
articles.summary: Crises upend plans, force people to re-evaluate their priorities, and bring into focus new goals. Financial markets give us hints of what we can expect from the aftermath of Covid-19.

articles.title: A place in the sun
articles.summary: While rents have been soaring for years in urban areas around the world, one Australian city has weathered the storm. What can the world learn from the experiences of Sydney?

articles.title: What ails the social sciences
articles.summary: Bad incentives, muddled theory and no practical use. The condition of the social sciences has been blamed on a great variety of things; what’s really at fault and how do we know?

articles.title: In praise of pastiche
articles.summary: Building traditionalist architecture today is derided as inauthentic pastiche. But this perspective turns a blind eye to the dramatic and sophisticated ways that design has been applied throughout history.

articles.title: Clusters rule everything around me
articles.summary: Some of the greatest advances in technology have emerged from bringing intelligent people together to solve problems. How do tech clusters develop & how can we use them to replicate past successes?

articles.title: Seeing on the far side of the moon
articles.summary: Electrical interference has restricted what humans can observe with telescopes. To make leaps as a species, now’s the time for us to build a telescope on the far side of the moon.

articles.title: Escaping science’s paradox
articles.summary: Scientific research today is afflicted by poor reliability and low utility, despite the best efforts of individual researchers.

articles.title: Innovation is not linear
articles.summary: Many have argued that innovation develops in a simple linear fashion – from research to experimentation to engineering.

articles.title: Securing posterity
articles.summary: New technologies can be dangerous, threatening the very survival of humanity. Is economic growth inherently risky, and how do we maximize the chances of a flourishing future?

articles.title: Interview: Mike Solana on tech and its critics
articles.summary: In spite of major technological progress, tech is often envisioned in the media with pessimism and dread.

articles.title: The daily grind
articles.summary: Before grinding mills were invented, the preparation of flour for food was an arduous task largely carried out by women for hours every day. How did it affect their lives and why does it remain a tradition in some places even today?

articles.title: Build state capacity by building charter cities
articles.summary: Many low-income countries are unable to provide effective governance for their citizens, trapped in a cycle of slow growth and persistent corruption. Charter cities may provide an answer.

articles.title: Practical veganism
articles.summary: Polls show that the majority of Americans want to reduce their consumption of meat, but many struggle to do so.

articles.title: The evolution of psychiatry
articles.summary: Modern psychiatry appears to be at a standstill, wanting for better treatment and a substantive theoretical framework. Evolutionary theory has the potential to reinvigorate the field.

articles.title: How to build a state
articles.summary: Throughout history, states struggled to maintain power, having to rely on private agents and enforcers to fund themselves and govern their citizens.

articles.title: Epidemic disease and the state
articles.summary: Western democracies appear to have floundered in their responses to Covid-19. Is there a relationship between health and freedom, and can it be unraveled?

articles.title: The rise and fall of the industrial R&D lab
articles.summary: For a time in recent history, R&D labs seemed to exist in a golden age of innovation and productivity. But this period vanished as swiftly as it came to be.

articles.title: The story of Viktor Zhdanov
articles.summary: Although Viktor Zhdanov’s name is little known today, he spearheaded one of the greatest projects in history. Who was he and what did he do?

articles.title: Unscrupulous Flattery
articles.summary: (A footnote on Crabtree, Kern, and Siegel, “ Cults of Personality, Preference Falsification, and the Dictator’s Dilemma ”, Journal of Theoretical Politics 32(3) 409-434 [2020]) Charles Crabtree, Holger Kern, and David Siegel just published an article in the Journal of Theoretical Politics that models cults of personality as screening devices . Their argument is similar to arguments I’ve made in th
articles.author: Xavier Marquez

articles.title: Dictators
articles.summary: In my corner of Twitter, I recently became obliquely aware of a kerfuffle about Michael Bloomberg saying that “Xi Jinping is not a dictator” . (Twitter is funny that way: sometimes all you get is a trace of some public person’s comment as reflected in memes or snark, and it takes some effort to find the original motivation for the jokes. It’s like a cloud chamber for political events). Not that th
articles.author: Xavier Marquez
